record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Recorder (county-level from formation; state registration 1947)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1911 (births and deaths; compliance uneven in early years, especially rural counties)

burned_county_authority: Idaho has no officially designated 'burned counties.' Some remote mountain counties have thin early records due to sparse population, difficult terrain, and the transient nature of mining camp populations. The Idaho State Historical Society (ISHS) and State Archives in Boise serve as the central repository for territorial and state records.
administrative_notes: Idaho Territory was organized in 1863 from parts of Washington, Dakota, and Nebraska territories. Idaho was admitted as the 43rd state on July 3, 1890. The original territorial counties included Ada, Boise, Idaho, Kootenai, Nez Perce, Oneida, Owyhee, and Shoshone. Idaho has 44 counties. The state capital moved from Lewiston to Boise in 1865. County Recorders maintain deed, land, and marriage records. Clerks of District Court handle probate and civil/criminal records. The Idaho State Historical Society in Boise is the central repository for state and territorial government records, microfilmed county records, mining company records, and historical manuscripts. Southeastern Idaho was heavily settled by LDS (Mormon) colonists from Utah beginning in 1860, and LDS ward records are primary genealogical sources for that region. The Nez Perce, Shoshone-Bannock, Coeur d'Alene, Kootenai, and Shoshone-Paiute tribes have reservations in Idaho.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Idaho has no burned counties. While some territorial-era and frontier mining camp records are sparse due to transient populations, remote terrain, and minimal early government infrastructure, this reflects settlement conditions rather than catastrophic record loss. County records generally survive from the date of county organization. The Idaho State Historical Society holds microfilm copies of many county records as a preservation backup.

core_repositories.source_name: Idaho State Historical Society (ISHS) and State Archives
core_repositories.description: Central state repository for Idaho records. Houses territorial and state government records, governor's papers, legislative records, mining company records, microfilmed county records (deeds, marriages, probate), livestock brand books, WPA records, extensive photographic collections, manuscript collections, and the state vital records index. The Idaho State Archives (co-located) holds state agency records, penitentiary records (1864+), and land records. The primary starting point for Idaho genealogical research.

Statewide genealogical society that publishes the Idaho Genealogical Society Quarterly, maintains research guides and county-specific resources, and coordinates with local genealogy societies across Idaho.

core_repositories.source_name: University of Idaho Library — Special Collections
core_repositories.description: Archives and Special Collections hold extensive Idaho manuscript collections including timber industry records (Potlatch Corporation, Boise Cascade), mining company records, Idaho political papers, Nez Perce tribal history materials, and regional photograph collections. Day of Remembrance Collection documents Japanese-American internment.

Holds federal records for Idaho including General Land Office (GLO) homestead and preemption entries, Bureau of Indian Affairs records for Idaho reservations (Nez Perce, Coeur d'Alene, Kootenai), federal court records (including naturalization), Forest Service records for Idaho national forests, and military post records.

familysearch_collections.description: Microfilmed county records including deeds, marriages, probate, court records, and vital records. Many browse-only image collections from Idaho county recorders. Also includes indexed collections of Idaho vital records, cemetery records, tax rolls, and naturalization records. LDS ward records for southeastern Idaho are particularly extensive.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability charts, and links to digitized microfilm collections for Idaho.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Idaho newspapers including territorial-era papers, mining camp newspapers, and county weeklies. Coverage from the 1860s forward. The Idaho State Historical Society has additional digitized content through the Idaho Digital Newspaper Project.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Idaho State Historical Society Newspaper Archives
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: ISHS holds the largest collection of Idaho newspapers on microfilm. Includes territorial-era papers from mining camps (Idaho World, Owyhee Avalanche), county weeklies, and ethnic community newspapers. Interlibrary loan available for many titles.

military_records.description: Records from Idaho military installations including Boise Barracks (1863-1912), Fort Lapwai (1862-1884, Nez Perce War), Fort Hall (1870-1883), Fort Boise (1863-1912), Fort Sherman/Coeur d'Alene (1878-1900), and Camp Lyon (1865-1869, Owyhee mining district). Post returns, muster rolls, and correspondence document soldiers and their families. Nez Perce War (1877) records are extensive.

military_records.repository: Idaho State Historical Society / County Recorders
military_records.description: Idaho State Historical Society holds veterans' discharge papers (DD-214) filed with county recorders, veterans' grave registrations, and WPA veterans' graves survey from the 1930s-40s. Idaho State Veterans Cemetery records in Boise. Mountain Home Air Force Base (1943+) and Gowen Field (1941+) records at NARA document military personnel.

land_and_property.source_name: Bureau of Land Management — General Land Office Records
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Idaho including homestead, preemption, desert land, and timber culture entries. Searchable database covers all federal land transactions. Idaho was almost entirely public domain — GLO records are the primary source for first land ownership. The Desert Land Act, Carey Act, and Reclamation Act were especially important in arid southern Idaho.
County Recorders maintain grantor/grantee deed indexes and deed books from county formation. In-person access at each county recorder's office. Some counties have digitized indexes available online.

Irrigation transformed southern Idaho. Bureau of Reclamation records for the Minidoka Project, Boise Project, Arrowrock Dam, American Falls Dam, and other projects at NARA Denver include water rights applications, settler correspondence, and land sale records. Private irrigation company records (Twin Falls Land and Water Company, etc.) at ISHS document purchasers.

vital_records.source_name: Idaho Bureau of Vital Records and Health Statistics
vital_records.repository: Idaho Department of Health and Welfare, Boise
vital_records.description: State registration of births and deaths from 1911. Marriage records at state level from 1947 (earlier at county). Divorce records from 1947. Certified copies available by mail or in person. Some early records have gaps in rural and mountain counties.

County recorders recorded births, deaths, and marriages before state registration. Coverage varies by county — some have records from territorial formation. Marriage licenses at county level are often the earliest vital records available. Southeastern Idaho LDS ward records supplement or replace secular vital records for Mormon communities.

church_and_cemetery.description: Cemetery transcriptions compiled by DAR chapters, county historical societies, and WPA workers. ISHS holds WPA cemetery survey records. FindAGrave has extensive Idaho coverage. USGenWeb Tombstone Project includes some Idaho counties. Pioneer cemeteries in mining towns often predate county formation.

church_and_cemetery.repository: FamilySearch.org / LDS Church History Library, Salt Lake City
church_and_cemetery.description: LDS ward and stake records are primary genealogical sources for southeastern Idaho counties (Bannock, Bear Lake, Bingham, Bonneville, Caribou, Cassia, Clark, Franklin, Fremont, Jefferson, Madison, Oneida, Power, Teton). Ward records include membership, baptism, blessing, ordination, marriage, and death records. Many are indexed on FamilySearch. The LDS Church History Library in Salt Lake City holds original records.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Free genealogy resources organized by county. Includes transcribed records, cemetery readings, family histories, and links to county-specific resources contributed by volunteers.

research_guides_and_societies.repository: Basque Museum and Cultural Center, Boise
research_guides_and_societies.description: Documents the Basque immigrant community in Idaho and the American West. Collections include immigration records, boarding house registers, sheepherder employment records, family photographs, and oral histories. Essential for researching Basque families who came as sheepherders from the 1890s through the 1960s.

historical_counties_extinct.source_name: Alturas County (extinct 1895, split into Blaine, Lincoln, and Elmore)
historical_counties_extinct.repository: Idaho State Historical Society / Blaine County Recorder
historical_counties_extinct.description: Alturas County was created in 1864 and covered a large area of south-central Idaho during the Wood River mining boom. It was dissolved in 1895, with its territory divided among Blaine, Lincoln, and Elmore counties. Records transferred to successor counties. Researchers looking for Alturas County records should check Blaine County (Hailey) for the Wood River Valley area, Lincoln County (Shoshone) for the southern portion, and ISHS for territorial-era records.

historical_counties_extinct.source_name: Logan County (extinct 1886, became Alturas then Blaine/Lincoln)
historical_counties_extinct.description: Logan County existed briefly (1889) and was absorbed into Alturas County. Records, if any survived, would be at ISHS. Logan County's territory eventually became parts of Blaine and Lincoln counties.

Lah-Toh County was proposed but never formally organized. Any associated legislative records are at ISHS in the territorial and state legislative files. Researchers may encounter this name in historical references.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Nez Perce Agency Records — Bureau of Indian Affairs
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Seattle / BIA Nez Perce Agency, Lapwai
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Nez Perce Indian Reservation (Nez Perce, Lewis, Idaho, Clearwater counties) records include Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records under the Dawes Act, annuity rolls, school records (Lapwai, Fort Lapwai, Kamiah schools), and agency correspondence. The Nez Perce War of 1877 generated extensive military records at NARA. Spalding Mission records (1836+) are among Idaho's earliest written documents.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Fort Hall Agency
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Fort Hall Indian Reservation (Bannock, Bingham, Power counties) is home to the Shoshone-Bannock Tribes. BIA records include census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, school attendance (Fort Hall School), and agency correspondence at NARA Denver. The Lemhi Shoshone were removed to Fort Hall in 1907; removal records document affected families.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Seattle / BIA Coeur d'Alene Agency
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Coeur d'Alene Indian Reservation (Benewah, Kootenai counties) records include census rolls, allotment records, and school files at NARA Seattle. Sacred Heart Mission (DeSmet, est. 1842 by Jesuits) records at Gonzaga University document early tribal members and mixed-heritage families. The Coeur d'Alene Tribe historically occupied lands around Coeur d'Alene Lake.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Pacific Region (San Bruno) / BIA Western Shoshone Agency
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Duck Valley Indian Reservation (Owyhee County, ID and Elko County, NV) is home to the Shoshone-Paiute Tribes. BIA Western Shoshone Agency records include census rolls, allotment records, and school files. The reservation straddles the Idaho-Nevada border.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Seattle / County District Courts / ISHS
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records filed in Idaho district courts from territorial era forward. Many Idaho immigrants naturalized locally — court records contain declarations of intention and petitions for naturalization with biographical details. Mining counties (Shoshone, Owyhee, Boise) had high naturalization volumes. Post-1906 records at NARA.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Basque Museum and Cultural Center, Boise / ISHS
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Basque sheepherders began arriving in Idaho in the 1890s, primarily from the provinces of Bizkaia and Gipuzkoa. Boarding house registers (Basque hotels in Boise, Shoshone, Gooding), sheep company employment records, and immigration documents at the Basque Museum and Cultural Center in Boise and ISHS. The Basque community centered in Ada County but sheepherders worked ranges across southern Idaho.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Chinese Community Records — Idaho Mining Towns
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: ISHS / Idaho City Historical Foundation / University of Idaho
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Chinese miners were a significant presence in Idaho's gold rush communities from the 1860s through the 1890s, particularly at Idaho City, Warren, Pierce, and along the Salmon River. Chinese tax lists, census records, mining claims, cemetery records, and exclusion-era records at ISHS and NARA document this community. Idaho City's Chinese cemetery and the Warren Chinese community are well-documented.
